file_name: Musacchio and Maurmo - 2024 - An ensemble architecture for melanoma classification.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) implemented via MATLAB's Deep Network Designer with Transfer Learning (SqueezeNet architecture) to classify skin lesions as melanoma or non-melanoma. The model was trained on dermoscopic images from the ISIC archive and applied to macro and micro smartphone images of nevus formations to evaluate diagnostic accuracy in a dermatology context.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the acquisition of skin lesion images using smartphone macro and micro lenses, the training of a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) using Transfer Learning on the ISIC dataset, and the evaluation of diagnostic accuracy on new data. It includes specific methods, results (75% accuracy for micro images vs 63% for macro), and a discussion of findings, which characterizes it as an original research study rather than a review.

title: Macro vs Micro Skin Imaging: Finding an Affordable Approach for Dermatological Care Access in Rural/Remote Areas
journal: Collaboration across Disciplines for the Health of People, Animals and Ecosystems
brief_summary: This study investigated the use of low-cost smartphone clip-on macro and microscopic lenses as an affordable alternative to dermatoscopes for remote melanoma diagnosis in underserved areas. A Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) based on the SqueezeNet architecture was trained via transfer learning on dermoscopic images from the ISIC archive to classify skin lesions. The model was subsequently evaluated on 60 rotated images of nevus formations captured from a single subject using both lens types. The CNN achieved a diagnostic accuracy of 75% on microscopic images compared to 63% on macroscopic images, demonstrating superior performance with the micro lens. These findings suggest that smartphone-based microscopic imaging can enhance diagnostic accuracy for melanoma in remote healthcare settings lacking specialized equipment.

lesion_types_studied.details: Used as a target label in the binary classification task (melanoma vs. non-melanoma). The CNN was trained on 314 melanoma and 314 non-melanoma dermoscopic images from the ISIC archive. An additional test session used 50 melanoma and 50 non-melanoma formations from ISIC. The study aims to improve diagnostic accuracy for melanoma.

Used as the comparator class in the binary classification task (melanoma vs. non-melanoma). The CNN was trained on 314 non-melanoma dermoscopic images from the ISIC archive. An additional test session used 50 non-melanoma formations from ISIC.

The actual clinical entity of the images used in the primary experiment. A total of 15 nevus formations from one subject were photographed using macro and micro lenses. These images were labeled as melanoma or non-melanoma by the CNN. The paper notes these formations were confirmed to be nevus following dermatological evaluation.

all_task_types.target: Binary diagnostic labels: melanoma vs. non-melanoma
all_task_types.details: The study uses a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) trained on dermoscopic images from the ISIC archive to classify skin lesions into two discrete categories: melanoma and non-melanoma. The model was evaluated on macro and micro smartphone images of nevus formations, predicting these binary diagnostic labels. Performance was measured by the percentage of images labeled correctly (75% for micro, 63% for macro) and confidence percentages of the classification.

datasets_used.name: ISIC Archive (Training Data)
datasets_used.description: Dermoscopic images from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) open source archive, used for training the CNN via transfer learning. The dataset consisted of melanoma and non-melanoma lesions which were rotated at 90°, 180°, and 270° to augment the data.

use.training: Used to train the SqueezeNet CNN via transfer learning. The original 314 melanoma and 314 non-melanoma images were rotated to create 2512 images, split into 70% training, 20% validation, and 10% testing.
use.validation: Used for validation during the training process (20% of the augmented ISIC data).
use.testing: Used for testing during the training process (10% of the augmented ISIC data).
use.other: An additional test session was performed on a separate subset of the ISIC database containing 50 melanoma and 50 non-melanoma formations not included in the training data, achieving 78% accuracy.

datasets_used.name: Subject Nevus Formations (Macro/Micro Images)
datasets_used.description: Images of nevus formations captured from a single subject using smartphone clip-on macro and microscopic lenses. The formations were confirmed to be nevus following dermatological evaluation. Images were rotated to create a dataset of 60 images (15 formations x 4 orientations).

use.testing: Used to evaluate the diagnostic accuracy of the trained CNN on macro and micro images. The CNN labeled these images as melanoma or non-melanoma to assess performance.
use.other: Primary experimental data for comparing macro vs. micro imaging performance.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. It compared diagnostic accuracy (75% for micro images vs. 63% for macro images) and confidence percentages descriptively without statistical inference.

ISIC_Archive_Test_Subset.description: Additional test session on dermoscopic dataset (50 melanoma/50 non-melanoma formations) not included in training data.

Subject_Nevus_Formations_Micro.description: Microscopic images of 15 nevus formations from one subject (rotated to 60 images total), labeled by CNN.
Subject_Nevus_Formations_Micro.notes: CNN labeled microscopic images with higher accuracy compared to macro images. Mislabeled images had lower confidence rates compared to macro mislabels.

Subject_Nevus_Formations_Macro.description: Macroscopic images of 15 nevus formations from one subject (rotated to 60 images total), labeled by CNN.
Subject_Nevus_Formations_Macro.notes: Macro nevus photos were mislabeled with higher confidence rates in comparison to micro images; more than half of the mislabeled images had a confidence in label of over 90%.

architecture_used.pretraining: Transfer Learning process using dermoscopic images from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) open source archive (314 melanoma and 314 non-melanoma lesions).

architecture_details.implementation: Acquired using MATLAB Toolbox, specifically Deep Network Designer, following a Transfer Learning process.

training_details.dataset: ISIC archive (augmented to 2512 images via rotation at 90°, 180°, and 270°)

preprocessing_techniques: Image rotation at 90°, 180°, and 270° for data augmentation (applied to both ISIC training data and subject nevus images)
Use of transparent gel to capture color details in macro lens images
Use of camera zoom functionality for macro lens images

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Images from the ISIC archive (314 melanoma and 314 non-melanoma lesions) and the subject's nevus formations were rotated at 90°, 180°, and 270° to augment the dataset and provide different perspectives. This resulted in 2512 images for training/validation/testing and 60 images for the experimental test set.

conclusions: Smartphone-based imaging with specialized microscopic lenses has the potential to improve diagnostic accuracy for melanoma and other dermatological conditions in remote healthcare settings lacking specialized equipment like dermatoscopes.
The Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) demonstrated better performance on microscopic images (75% accuracy) compared to macroscopic images (63% accuracy) when classifying nevus formations.

Macroscopic images captured with smartphone clip-on lenses suffered from limitations such as shadow casting, instability due to zoom usage, and reduced color variation capture, whereas microscopic lenses provided better detail regarding color distribution and borders.
Mislabeled macro images were associated with higher confidence rates (more than half had confidence over 90%), indicating that low-quality images can lead to confident but incorrect AI predictions.
The study acknowledges limitations including the exclusive use of nevus formations from a single subject and the lack of collaboration with a dermatologist for ground truth labeling and validation.

Smartphones equipped with magnifying lenses can serve as an affordable alternative for teledermatology in underserved regions, facilitating remote diagnosis and longitudinal observation of skin lesions.
important_remarks: The study relies on images of nevus formations from a single subject, which severely limits the generalizability of the findings to the broader population.
Ground truth labeling was performed by the CNN itself rather than a dermatologist, as the authors explicitly state there was 'no collaboration with a dermatologist' for labeling or validation.
The model was trained on dermoscopic images from the ISIC archive but tested on macro/micro smartphone images of nevi, creating a significant domain shift between training and testing data.
The paper does not report any statistical significance tests (e.g., p-values) to support the observed difference in accuracy between macro (63%) and micro (75%) imaging.
The CNN demonstrated high confidence (over 90%) even when mislabeling macro images, indicating a risk of confident but incorrect predictions with lower-quality input data.
The dataset used for the primary experiment consisted of only 60 images (15 formations rotated 4 times), which is an extremely small sample size for evaluating diagnostic accuracy.
The study acknowledges that macro lens images suffered from shadow casting, instability due to zoom usage, and reduced color variation capture, which likely contributed to lower performance.

The test set for the primary comparison was not independent of the training distribution in terms of lesion type (nevi vs. melanoma/non-melanoma training data), as the CNN was trained to classify melanoma vs. non-melanoma but applied to nevi.
